title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku: Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Technology
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. They will learn about the traditional practices of Ngāti Kahungunu in monitoring and protecting waterways, and how these can be integrated with modern technology to address current environmental challenges facing our local stream ecosystem.
learningObjectives: Design and construct a simple water quality testing device using both traditional Māori knowledge and modern materials
Analyze water quality data from Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku to identify potential environmental concerns
Create a digital presentation that combines mātauranga Māori and technological solutions for stream restoration
Collaborate with local kaumātua to develop culturally-responsive technological approaches to waterway guardianship

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa
activities.description: Visit Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku with kaumātua to learn traditional methods of assessing water health through observation of native species, water clarity, and plant life. Students document observations using digital cameras and field journals.

activities.title: Hanga Pataka Raraunga Wai
activities.description: Design and build simple water testing tools using recycled materials to measure pH, turbidity, and temperature, incorporating traditional knowledge about natural indicators of water quality.

activities.title: Tātari me te Arotake
activities.description: Collect and analyze water samples from three different points along Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, comparing traditional assessment methods with technological measurements using digital data logging tools.

activities.title: Whakamahere Kaitiakitanga
activities.description: Work in small groups to develop technological solutions for identified stream issues, creating digital presentations that integrate mātauranga Māori with appropriate technologies for monitoring and restoration.

activities.title: Whakaaturanga ki te Hapori
activities.description: Present findings and proposed technological solutions to community members, incorporating feedback from kaumātua and local environmental experts to refine approaches.

resources: Kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu with knowledge of traditional water monitoring practices
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' water quality monitoring resources
Digital data loggers and water testing equipment from the college science department
Local stream restoration case studies from the Department of Conservation website

tasks: Design and construction of a functional water testing device
Data analysis report comparing traditional and technological assessment methods
Digital presentation proposing integrated technological solutions for stream restoration
Reflective journal documenting learning about kaitiakitanga principles

nzcAlignment: Technological Practice: Planning for practice, brief development, outcome development and evaluation
Nature of Technology: Characteristics of technology, characteristics of technological outcomes
Technological Knowledge: Technological modelling, technological products
Key Competencies: Participating and contributing, relating to others
